---
title: "4.6 Using Text Files"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Using Text Files

### Files let programs work with data that persists after the program stops

The textbook uses files as the bridge from hard-coded collections to real stored data.

::: {.tight-list}
- a <span class="term">file</span> stores data outside the running program
- the data can be loaded during execution
- this separates data from code and supports larger data sets
:::

## `File`, `Scanner`, and `IOException`

### Java needs both file access and input-reading tools

```java
import java.io.File;
import java.io.IOException;
import java.util.Scanner;
```

Core ideas retained from the textbook:

::: {.tight-list}
- `File` identifies the file to open
- `Scanner` reads the data
- invalid file names can trigger exceptions
- one common AP-level strategy is adding `throws IOException`
:::

## Reading Data with `Scanner`

### Different methods read different shapes of input

Important `Scanner` operations kept here:

::: {.tight-list}
- `nextLine()` reads an entire line
- `next()` reads the next token
- `nextInt()` and `nextDouble()` read numeric values
- `hasNext()` checks whether more input remains
- `close()` closes the file stream
:::

Mixing `nextLine()` with numeric reads often requires extra care because of leftover whitespace.

## Loop to Read a File

### Use a while loop until there is no more input

```java
while (scan.hasNext())
{
    String line = scan.nextLine();
}
scan.close();
```

This is the retained pattern for multi-line file processing.

## Save File Data into an Array

### Files can feed arrays or arrays of objects

The textbook keeps two stages together:

::: {.tight-list}
- read each line from the file
- store lines or extracted values into an array
- later move from raw text into object-oriented structures
:::

This topic also previews why `ArrayList` can be useful when the final size is not known in advance.

## Split Strings and CSV Data

### One line can contain several fields separated by a delimiter

```java
String[] data = line.split(",");
```

Retained ideas:

::: {.tight-list}
- CSV means <span class="term">Comma Separated Values</span>
- `split` breaks one line into fields
- the resulting array lets the program identify specific attributes by index
- string fields can later be converted into numbers if needed
:::

## Object-Oriented Design with CSV Files

### Split fields can become objects, not just raw strings

The textbook's final move for this topic is:

::: {.tight-list}
- define a class that matches the important columns
- split each line into attributes
- construct an object for that row
- store those objects in an array
:::

This is the clean bridge from input files to structured program design.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- fix file-reading code by adding `throws IOException`
- choose the right `Scanner` method for a given input type
- use a while loop with `hasNext()` to read a file
- split CSV lines into fields and identify important indices
- convert file rows into arrays or arrays of objects
- <span class="term">4.6.7 Coding Challenge: Array of Pokemon from Input File</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- define a file as persistent storage
- explain the roles of `File`, `Scanner`, and `IOException`
- describe the standard while-loop pattern for file reading
- explain what `split` does for CSV data
- explain how one row of file data can become one object
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
